items.q: If two variables are strongly correlated, what can you conclude?
items.correct: They vary together — but correlation alone does not establish that one causes the other.
items.misconception: Strong correlation means that one variable causes the other.
items.random_wrong: Strong correlation means the variables are equal in value.
items.why: Students conflate correlation with causation because in many physical contexts they coincide — but in stats this conflation is a classic error.

items.q: If a data set has an extreme outlier, which average is most affected?
items.correct: The mean — because every value (including the outlier) contributes to it.
items.misconception: The median is most affected because the outlier shifts the middle.
items.random_wrong: The mode is most affected because the outlier becomes the new most common value.
items.why: Students don't distinguish how each average is computed; they think any extreme value disturbs all measures equally.

items.q: What does a larger standard deviation tell you about a data set?
items.correct: The data values are more spread out around the mean.
items.misconception: The mean of the data set is larger.
items.random_wrong: There are more data points in the set.
items.why: Students confuse standard deviation (a measure of spread) with the mean (a measure of centre) since both are summary statistics.
It's -1 to 1. “0 to 1” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's No linear correlation. “Perfect correlation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Predict y at x=2 from y=5+2x
items.why: Exact answer: 9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
